header.tagline: Reading the Pacific through its data

home.subtitle: The Pacific lives climate change before the rest of the world — and it documents it, number by number. A sea that rises, that warms, islands on the front line, and a region that refuses to just take it.
home.live_loading: Connecting to the Pacific Data Hub…
home.acts_intro: Twelve acts to understand it. A region already on the move.

acts.a1_text: Fifty years of data, one constant: the Pacific's per-capita carbon footprint stays light, and stays light over time. This act measures it territory by territory — the bedrock of the story.
acts.a2_text: Sea-surface temperature anomaly, territory by territory: the gap to the normal, year after year. Knowing how to read the ocean means being able to anticipate — for the living world, the sky and the cyclone seasons of the acts ahead.
acts.a3_title: Where the sea meets the people
acts.a3_text: Two official datasets crossed: the sea-level anomaly per EEZ (1993–2012 reference) and the population growth rate. Knowing precisely where the water rises and where people live: the first tool of adaptation planning — the very reason this data exists territory by territory.
acts.a4_text: Two official Sendai Framework indicators (UNDRR): people affected by disasters and direct economic losses. Recording precisely what strikes, where and when — the basis of all preparedness and of regional solidarity.
acts.a5_text: Two complementary official datasets: the renewables share in final energy consumption (UN, in %) and electricity production broken down by source (SPC, in GWh, from solar to hydro to fossil). One gives the share, the other says where each kilowatt-hour comes from.
acts.a6_text: Two official datasets: crop and livestock yields (FAOSTAT, kg/ha and kg/animal) and the CALCI land-cover index (IMF, base 100 in 2015). The official examples point the right way — Tonga: 6,114 → 7,325 kg/ha in twenty years — and every crop keeps its own story.
acts.a7_text: Two official datasets side by side: the Red List Index (IUCN/BirdLife, SDG 15.5.1, from 0 to 1) estimating extinction risk, and the count of fisheries-management measures in force (FAOLEX). On one side what we seek to protect, on the other what we put in place to do it.
acts.a8_text: Three official datasets for one sky: rainfall against its 1991–2020 normal, land temperatures against the 1971–2000 normal — and the network of weather stations that, year after year, makes measuring them possible at all.
acts.a9_title: Reading the economy against the climate
acts.a9_text: Two official datasets, fully exploited: visitor arrivals (UN Tourism, in counts, down to tourists / same-day visitors) and environmental taxation (IMF ↠ OECD, as % of GDP, split into energy, transport, pollution, resources). A resource to manage, a lever taking hold.
acts.a10_text: Two official UN indicators: the share of population with safely managed drinking water (WHO/UNICEF, in %, where rising is progress) and tuberculosis incidence (WHO, cases/100,000, where falling is progress). Two opposite reading directions — and examples that point the right way.
acts.a11_text: Everything crossed: responsibility and vulnerability, to light where the Pacific is advancing and where to focus effort.
acts.a12_text: Forty-seven seasons of tropical tracks sweeping across New Caledonia and the south-west Pacific.

home.thesis: Twelve acts to tell the Pacific's climate story differently: not a fate to endure, but a region that measures, adapts and gets ahead — with nothing but its own data as the source.
home.shock_text: of global emissions: that's the Pacific's entire weight in the crisis. And yet it's one of the voices that carry furthest. Here, being on the front line doesn't mean playing the victim — it means leading the way.
home.acts_lead: Every act starts from a verifiable number and follows it all the way: the reality it describes, and the response the Pacific is already mounting. From emissions to solutions, the thread never leaves the ground — and it leads to action.
home.closing: “Where the world sees a threat, the Pacific has already begun charting the way out.”
home.c1_desc: We start with the facts, unfiltered: what the Pacific emits, what the ocean sends back, the pressure mounting on its people. Clean numbers — because no solution holds on a blurry diagnosis.
home.c2_desc: Then we read the response in the data: energy, agriculture, biodiversity, atmosphere, economy. Five fronts where the Pacific doesn't just hold the line — it reinvents how it adapts.
home.c3_desc: We close in closest to people — water, health — before the synthesis that calls it: what's genuinely advancing, and where to focus the effort next.
home.stat4_text: to read a region that adapts
home.m1_desc: Who emits what, per capita and territory by territory. The Pacific median sets the benchmark, and every gap gets its explanation — responsibility laid out before anything else.
home.m2_desc: The signals the climate sends, and that we actually measure: sea-surface temperature, rainfall and heat anomalies, the observation network. Disruption in data, not in impressions.
home.m3_desc: What the climate does to what feeds and surrounds us: crop yields, species-extinction risk, fisheries management. The whole web of life, under strain.
home.m4_desc: Where the figures meet faces: the sea against inhabited coasts, water access, disease, the toll of disasters. Climate at the scale of daily life.
home.m5_desc: The levers that work — renewable share, economic shift — then the synthesis that sets responsibility against vulnerability. Time for the reckoning, and the decisions.

intro.lead: The Pacific is on the front line of warming.
intro.body1: Built as a website in several acts, the platform draws on as much open data as possible to shed light on every facet of the regional climate challenge: rising seas, a warming ocean, food autonomy, health, the economy.
intro.body2: The aim is not only to document, but to understand the mechanisms and open paths to solutions for the peoples of the Pacific — the most exposed, and already on the move.
intro.p1_text: Open, real, verifiable data across the 22 territories.
intro.p2_text: Twelve acts connecting the figures to realities lived on the ground.
intro.p3_text: Giving decision-makers and communities what they need to build solutions.

guide.title: How to read this
guide.intro: This act reads a single official Challenge dataset: greenhouse gas emissions per capita (t CO₂e/capita, annual series from the Pacific Data Hub). Per-capita values are computed at the source; we reformat them and never transform them. Every view below is another way of reading the same series.

steps.v: The subregion pills (Melanesia / Polynesia / Micronesia) refocus every per-territory view. “All” keeps the 17.

Each bar is a territory (SPC code). Longer means a higher per-capita footprint. A per-capita rate divides emissions by a population: on an island of a few thousand people, one diesel power plant is enough to send the ratio jumping — the denominator effect behind the extremes (Palau, New Caledonia).
The line marks the Pacific median: the value splitting territories into two equal halves. We prefer it to the mean, which a handful of extreme values would skew.
The scatter crosses our emissions series (x) with the sea-level rise measured per EEZ in Act 2 (y): two official datasets answering each other — and giving the word “sobriety” its full weight.

The data covers CO₂, converted to CO₂-equivalent using IPCC (AR5) global-warming potentials — not the six Kyoto gases. Estimated precision: 4–35% at national level. Practical consequence: we read trends and orders of magnitude, not the second decimal.

guide.takeaway: Takeaway: a historic sobriety, held across fifty years of data — the Pacific enters the transition with a head start, not a debt.

story.rank: The vast majority of territories sit below the regional median: sobriety is the norm, the exceptions can be counted on one hand.
story.trend: Contained trajectories across half a century: the region develops without its per-capita footprint taking off — a quiet but real decoupling.
story.map: From one archipelago to the next, the per-capita footprint stays modest; the rare peaks read through their denominator (tiny populations, diesel).
story.heat: Read at a glance, the territories × years matrix confirms durably low intensity — few hot bands, plenty of steadiness.
story.sub: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia share a sober profile; their moderate gaps open the way to mutual learning between archipelagos.
story.change: Between the first and last year of data, most territories are stable or declining: sobriety holds over time.
story.box: Each box sums up a year: the median and the central 50% of territories. The band stays low and tight; every outlying point can be explained one by one.
story.river: Seen as a stacked flow, the regional profile stays steady year after year: no drift — a stable footing for the rest of the story.
story.tree: Tile area shows the footprint for the chosen year, grouping shows the subregion: most of the mosaic stays in sober tones.
story.race: Fast-forwarded across half a century, the ranking barely moves: sobriety is not a snapshot, it is a sustained trajectory.
story.bump: Beyond the values, the ranks are remarkably stable: each territory holds its emissions profile over time.
story.radar: Decade after decade, the three subregions keep a tight polygon: half a century of steadiness, readable in a single shape.
story.scatter: Bottom-left of the cloud: a light footprint, trending down — that is where most Pacific territories gather.

f.color_help: By rank: colour follows the ranking (every territory stands out). Absolute: colour follows the raw value (an extreme can flatten everything).

outro.title: A head start worth defending
outro.text: Act 1 establishes a fact: the Pacific's per-capita footprint is light, and has stayed light. The journey now turns to what the ocean is already recording — on to Act 2.

act1.thesis: Across the Pacific, per-capita footprints vary tenfold between territories — and the data explains every gap, denominator included.

board.rank_take: Sobriety is the rule; the peaks are explained (tiny populations, diesel, industry) rather than extrapolated.

board.trend_take: No collective surge: the Pacific's development was not paid for in per-capita carbon.

board.map_find: The geography of the per-capita footprint, year after year, across the Pacific territories.
board.map_take: The peaks sit in small territories: a denominator effect, not a region losing its grip.
board.heat_find: Each row a territory, each column a year: half a century of intensity in a single glance.
board.heat_take: Plenty of steadiness, few sustained episodes: the data tells a story of stability.
board.change_find: Net change in per-capita footprint between the first and last year of data.

act2.lead: One physical signal in this act: the sea-surface temperature anomaly (°C). 0 = the reference normal; above = warmer, below = cooler. Following that gap year after year means reading the ocean before reading its consequences (Acts 7, 8 and 12).
act2.coming: Data retrieved ✓ — visualisations in preparation. The full page comes next.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: This act reads a single official Challenge dataset: the sea-surface temperature anomaly, by territory and year. An anomaly compares the ocean to itself — the gap to a reference period — not an absolute temperature. Values are reformatted, never transformed.

steps.v: 0 = the reference normal. +0.4 does not mean “the water was 0.4 °C”: it was 0.4 °C above its local normal. Each territory is compared to itself.

0 = the 1971–2000 climatological normal (NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0). The monthly anomaly is averaged within each EEZ then aggregated to an annual mean — we recompute nothing: the chart's 0 line is the producer's normal.
The Pacific breathes naturally (El Niño / La Niña): one isolated year does not make a trend. The fair read is where the last few years sit against 0 — not a single peak.
In the ranking and on the map, the line and the mid tint mark 0: everything reads as a gap, above or below the normal.

The native grid is coarse (5° × 5°): its alignment to EEZs is refined geometrically, without gaining real resolution — thermal homogeneity is assumed within each original cell. Land masking may exclude some littoral zones around complex coasts. And on our side: no smoothing, no correction, no filling (see Coverage).

guide.takeaway: Takeaway: you read a position against the normal, not a temperature — and you judge over time, never on a single year.

story.band: The line tracks the territories' mean; the band, the min–max spread. The right question: where does the curve sit against 0 over the last few years — and is the band tightening or widening?
story.rank: The chosen year's ranking is a snapshot. Switch years with the map or the matrix before concluding — the film informs, not the photo.
story.map: The geography of the gap, year by year. Scrub through: some zones oscillate, others sit durably on one side of 0.
story.heat: Each row a territory, each column a year: the matrix tells at a glance what oscillates from what settles in.
story.corr: Territories rising most are often those warming most — two signals of one disruption.
story.box: Year after year the distribution shifts upward: median and extremes rise.
story.river: The three sub-regions follow the same upward slope, at similar rates.
story.change: First year joined to the last, territory by territory: the sign gives the direction, the size gives the distance — to be read with the caution anomaly series deserve.
story.dual: Both lines rise together: the Pacific warms as it rises. Two measures, one disruption.
story.share: Each year: the share of territories above their normal. While it oscillates, the ocean breathes; when it sits high durably, the gap is no longer local.
story.dumb: The muted dot marks the first year, the warm dot the last: the gap is the distance travelled.

outro.title: An ocean we know how to read
outro.text: Oscillation on one side, trend on the other: the gap to the normal holds no more secrets. The sky tells what follows — rainfall and temperatures against their normals, in Act 8.

act2.thesis: The gap to the normal, territory by territory: a simple read — one that teaches you to tell a single year's swing from a decade's trend.

spotlight.r_src_v: NOAA / NCEI — NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 · via Pacific Data Hub (.Stat), official Challenge dataset
spotlight.r_meas_v: Sea-surface temperature anomaly, EEZ spatial mean
spotlight.n1: An anomaly compares the ocean to itself: the gap to the 1971–2000 climatological normal, not an absolute temperature.
spotlight.n2: Native 5° × 5° grid, geometrically refined to align with EEZs — with no gain in real resolution; a standard error accompanies each value as the uncertainty metric.
spotlight.n3: The Pacific oscillates naturally (El Niño / La Niña): one isolated year does not make a trend.
spotlight.n4: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years.
spotlight.ex_text: Palau EEZ, 1991: −0.35 °C — a surface cooler than its 1971–2000 normal. 2025: +0.92 °C — warmer than the normal. Two values, and the distance travelled reads by itself.

act3.lead: While the sea departs from its reference, the human map is being redrawn. Reading both together — where the water rises, where people live and arrive — turns two official series into a tool for anticipation.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: This act crosses two official Challenge datasets — the sea-level anomaly (Copernicus, EEZ mean) and the population growth rate (SPC) — completed by a static coastline aggregate (Digital Earth Pacific). Three sources, three records, one rule: cross to locate, never to conclude.

steps.v: The gap, in metres, between observed sea-surface height and the 1993–2012 long-term reference, averaged within each EEZ. 0 = the reference; above = higher. Years with fewer than 12 months of data are flagged incomplete.

steps.k: Population: a rate, not a headcount
steps.v: [births − deaths + net migration] / population × 100, in %. 0 = stable population; negative = decline (emigration included). The annual rate rests on censuses run every 5 to 10 years, completed by models and projections between two collections.
The scatter places each territory by its sea (x) and its demography (y). It locates situations — it demonstrates no mechanism. A prioritisation tool, not a proof.

A complementary layer (Landsat, Digital Earth Pacific, CC BY-NC): retreat and advance are both measured. A static aggregate, an approximate read at territory level — its source record details method and thresholds.

Sea trends vary from one EEZ to another (temperature, salinity, local circulations): precisely what makes territory-by-territory monitoring indispensable for planning. And on our side: reformatting only — no smoothing, no filling.

guide.takeaway: Takeaway: crossing locates — it does not conclude. These views point to where to look closely, which is exactly what adaptation planning requires.

board.cross_take: Top right, the sea rises and the population still grows: that is where planning looks first.
board.band_find: Mean sea-level anomaly and the spread across territories, year by year.
board.band_take: The fair read: the curve's position against the 1993–2012 reference, over time — one year's swing is not a decade's settling-in.
board.pop_find: Latest population growth rate, territory by territory.
board.pop_take: Contrasting dynamics — and known ones: where population still grows, the information arrives in time to plan.
board.profile_find: Relative rank (0–100) for each territory: sea level (left) against the population growth rate (right).
board.profile_take: Long bars on both sides flag the most exposed: high sea AND a still-growing population.
board.path_find: For each territory, the growth rate from the first observed year (dull dot) to the most recent (warm dot).
board.path_take: Most trajectories slide downward: a demographic transition, documented territory by territory.
board.coast_find: Each dot is a satellite-tracked coastline segment (Landsat, 1999–2023): red is retreat, blue is growth, expressed in metres per year.
board.coast_take: Retreat is not uniform along the coast: it concentrates on specific stretches, often among the most inhabited.
board.coastbal_find: For each territory, the share of coastline retreating (red, left) versus growing (blue, right), aggregated from satellite-tracked segments.
board.coastbal_take: French Polynesia, American Samoa and Papua New Guinea lean toward retreat; conversely, several atolls (Tuvalu, Federated States of Micronesia) are gaining ground — erosion is not a uniform fate.
board.coastmap_find: Per-territory map: column height and colour intensity show the share of coastline in retreat.
board.coastmap_take: Retreat hotspots concentrate on a few territories; elsewhere the coast stays stable or advances.
board.slope_find: For each territory, sea level at the first and last observed year (mm anomaly). A rising slope = rising sea.
board.slope_take: Each slope is the gap between two real years, in millimetres: the overall direction reads itself, and every exception deserves its own look.
board.spread_find: Each box summarises a territory's distribution of coastline-change rates (median, quartiles, extremes), in m/year.
board.spread_take: A single coast often mixes retreat and growth: the spread reveals the heterogeneity that averages hide.
board.bubble_find: Cross-view: x = population growth, y = share of coastline retreating, bubble size = number of tracked segments.
board.bubble_take: Where population grows fast and the coast retreats, pressures compound — those are the territories to watch first.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two crossed official datasets — sea and population — plus the coastline note: sources, references, formulas, and what crossing means.
board.read_take: An act that crosses datasets starts by showing both ID cards: the condition of an honest crossing.
board.coverage_find: Each cell says whether a sea-level value exists for that territory in that year — including years the producer flags as incomplete.
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where the series ends: this act's signature, like every other.

spotlight.n2: Sea: trends vary from one EEZ to another (temperature, salinity, circulations) — which is what makes territory-level monitoring indispensable for adaptation planning.
spotlight.n3: Population: the annual rate rests on censuses (every 5 to 10 years), completed by models and projections between collections — worth keeping in mind for fine variations.
spotlight.n4: Crossing two datasets proves no cause: the scatter locates situations, it demonstrates no mechanism.
spotlight.n5: Coastline: a static Digital Earth Pacific aggregate (Landsat, CC BY-NC), an approximate territory-level read — method and thresholds detailed in its source record.
spotlight.ex_text: Northern Mariana Islands EEZ, 1993: −0.02 m — two centimetres below the 1993–2012 reference. 2013: +0.06 m — six centimetres above. One EEZ, twenty years, eight centimetres travelled.

act4.timeline_sub: one circle per event, sized by magnitude

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: Here the data isn't a steady trend but a series of DISASTERS: isolated spikes, many years with no event. So we look at totals and shocks, not a curve.

steps.v: Each disaster is a dated event: people affected and economic losses cluster in a few years.
Each circle is an event placed on its year; the bigger it is, the more people it affected (or the more it cost). Clusters reveal the dark years.
The total over the whole period shows which territories carry the heaviest toll.
Heights are on a logarithmic scale: without it, Fiji would dwarf everyone else. Taller and redder means a higher total.

guide.takeaway: A handful of events — Winston (Fiji, 2016), Pam (Vanuatu, 2015) — concentrate most of the human cost: hundreds of thousands of people affected and hundreds of millions of dollars in losses.

act4.lead: When a hazard strikes, it leaves a recorded trace: people affected and direct economic losses. The Sendai Framework (UNDRR) catalogues these traces country by country, event by event — to understand, compare and prepare better.
act4.dual_sub: people affected (left) and losses (right), per year

story.timeline: Each circle is a disaster: few events, but some enormous. The rhythm is nothing like a smooth curve.
story.annual: Aggregated by year, the spikes stand out: a few years concentrate most of the damage.
story.dual: Human cost and economic cost often rise together — two faces of one shock.
story.rank: Over the whole period, a few territories pile up a far heavier toll than the rest.
story.pareto: The cumulative line climbs fast: a handful of territories makes up most of the total.
story.treemap: Area shows the share: at a glance, you see who takes the most.
story.heat: The grid reveals the one-off shocks — when and where they struck, beyond the totals.
story.map: On the map, the cumulative footprint reads in relief: a logarithmic scale so small territories aren't crushed.

outro.title: Counting to prepare
outro.text: People affected, reported losses: an imperfect but precious record that grounds preparedness. Now to see how the region moves — the momentum.

act4.thesis: People affected and direct economic losses from disasters, reported by national focal points in the Sendai Framework Monitoring system. Two official UN indicators, to be read for what they are: a declarative record, valuable but uneven across countries.

board.timeline_find: Each circle a disaster, placed at its year; the radius shows the scale.
board.timeline_take: Each circle is a reported event, placed at its year: the timeline shows the scatter of disasters, without inferring a trend from a single point.
board.annual_find: Yearly total impact: the trend shows whether recent years weigh more.
board.annual_take: The annual total depends as much on events as on how complete the reporting was that year: you're reading a record, not an exhaustive measurement.
board.rank_find: Cumulative total per territory over the whole period (root scale keeps small ones visible).
board.rank_take: The per-territory total reflects exposure as much as recording quality: a low total may also signal incomplete reporting.
board.map_find: The geography of cumulative impact across the archipelago (log scale).
board.map_take: The geography of the recorded impact, to be read with coverage in mind: missing data is not the absence of an event.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two Sendai Framework indicators — people affected and economic losses — with their definitions, declarative sources and limits.
board.read_take: A declarative record is not an exhaustive count: saying so is the condition for reading these figures without over-interpreting them.
board.coverage_find: For the chosen measure: each cell says whether a reported value exists for that territory in that year. The gaps — many here — are shown as is, never filled.
board.coverage_take: Seeing where reporting exists and where it's missing: essential for a declarative dataset like this one.

act4.coverage_title: Coverage of the chosen measure: what is reported — and what isn't

spotlight.r1v: UNDRR · Sendai Framework, indicator B / SDG 11.5.1 (VC_DSR_AFFCT) · reported by national focal points · via the Pacific Data Hub
spotlight.r2v: Number of people affected by disasters · national reporting, by event and by year
spotlight.r3v: UNDRR · Sendai Framework, Target C / SDG 11.5.2 · average annual loss (VC_DSR_AALT) · via the Pacific Data Hub · upd. 2024-12-20
spotlight.r4v: Current US$ · DIRECT economic loss = monetary value of total or partial destruction of physical assets (homes, schools, hospitals, infrastructure, crops, livestock…)
spotlight.n1: Both datasets are DECLARATIVE: they come exclusively from official national reporting (Sendai Framework Monitoring system and DesInventar-Sendai databases), via designated focal points.
spotlight.n2: DIRECT loss ≠ indirect loss: it measures the physical damage occurring during the event or within the following hours (tangible, relatively easy to assess), not the later economic slowdown.
spotlight.n3: Uneven coverage: international sources often record only events above an impact threshold, with non-uniform methods — hence heterogeneous series and many years without data (see Coverage).
spotlight.n4: Two different units: a number of people doesn't compare to dollars. The measure selector changes the unit, format and scale of each view.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no imputation or filling on our side — a missing report stays missing, never a zero.
spotlight.ex_text: Losses: Fiji reported US$141.4M average annual loss for 2011 and US$374.1M for 2014 — two years, two very different magnitudes. And for many country-years, the cell is empty: not zero, but no report.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: The final act, and it looks ahead: the share of renewables in final energy consumption (%). Here, higher is better.

steps.v: 0% = all fossil; 100% = fully renewable energy. The bottom reference (0) is the starting point, not a normal.
The thick line is the regional average; the band shows where 8 in 10 territories sit (10th–90th percentile).
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on the curve AND the green 3D map.
The taller and greener a column, the higher the renewable share. The podium ranks the year's champions.

guide.takeaway: The momentum is real: driven by hydro, biomass and now solar, the renewable share is climbing — Kiribati, Fiji, Vanuatu, Samoa and the larger Melanesian islands leading the way.

act5.evo_sub: change in share between first and last year
act5.lead: Two official views of the same transition: the share of renewables in final energy consumption (in %), and electricity production broken down by source (in GWh). The first summarises, the second decomposes — where the power actually comes from, source by source.
act5.gauge_sub: average renewable share of territories, in the current year
act5.change_sub: change in share between the first and last year
act5.dumb_sub: first → last observed year, per territory
act5.heat_sub: renewable share — the greener, the higher

story.trend: The regional mean climbs; the band shows the gap between pioneers and laggards.
story.gauge: In one figure, the region's renewable momentum — slide through the years to watch it rise.
story.map: On the map, green gains ground: some territories already draw most of their energy from renewables.
story.rank: In the chosen year, the champions' podium — often small territories with strong hydro/solar potential.
story.change: Not everyone moves at the same pace: a few territories leap ahead, others stall.
story.dumb: The distance travelled from one end of the period to the other: most segments stretch upward.
story.heat: Green intensifies from left to right: the transition reads at a glance.
story.evo: The strongest gains, in percentage points added.
story.table: Every value, sortable, to explore territory by territory.

outro.title: Momentum, measured
outro.text: A share that swings, a production that diversifies: two indicators read for what they are, without predicting what's next. Now to what carries this energy: the economy.

act5.thesis: Renewables share in final consumption (%, SDG 7.2.1) and electricity production by source (GWh, SPC). Two official indicators of different natures: an estimated percentage and reported volumes. Read side by side, without confusing a share with a quantity.

board.trend_find: Renewable share of total final energy consumption (all uses: electricity, cooking, heating, transport). The band shows the regional spread (10–90%) around the mean.
board.trend_take: The fair read happens over time: the renewable share swings year to year (hydro depends on rainfall) — it's the trend, not a peak, that informs.
board.lines_find: Each line is a territory: renewable share of final energy consumption — who takes off, who stalls.
board.lines_take: Each territory has its own trajectory: small islands often start very low, large ones rely on hydro. Read each at home, with no flattening mean.
board.rank_find: Renewable share of final energy consumption, territory by territory, year after year: the bars reorder over time.
board.rank_take: The ranking reshuffles over the years: what the race shows is relative movement, not a destination.
board.map_find: Geography of the renewable share in final energy consumption for the selected year (green = greener).
board.map_take: The geography of the renewable share at the chosen year: a snapshot — the dynamics read in the trend, not in a single map.
board.evo_find: Who progressed most (and who fell back) on their renewable share of final energy consumption since the start.
board.evo_take: Period-to-period changes read territory by territory; a sharp rise says a local change, not a regional rule.
board.mix_band_find: Electricity generation (GWh) stacked, fossil against renewable, by year.
board.mix_band_take: Fossil and renewable side by side, by volume: the composition reads directly, without extrapolating what comes next.
board.mix_detail_find: Electricity output by source over the years (stacked columns); the total on top shows demand growth.
board.mix_detail_take: The per-source detail says what actually produces the power: hydro, solar, fossil — each in its place.
board.mix_compo_find: Share of each source in every territory's generation (100% bars), for the selected year.
board.mix_compo_take: Composition differs sharply across territories: geography, resources and local choices show through.
board.mix_share_find: Renewable share of each territory's electricity generation, ranked, for the selected year.
board.mix_share_take: The ranking reveals the transition pioneers and those still locked into thermal power.
board.mix_race_find: Animated progression of the renewable share (%) in each territory's electricity generation, year by year.
board.mix_race_take: The transition plays out live: who pulls ahead, who catches up, who flips to clean power.
board.mix_tree_find: Share of each source in the selection's total electricity generation (area ∝ output), for the slider year.
board.mix_tree_take: Oil's weight is obvious; clean sources still cover only a fraction of the area.
board.mix_donut_find: Electricity mix composition of one territory (or region) for a given year: pick the area and year. The centre shows the renewable share.
board.mix_donut_take: Source by source, you see exactly what each island's power depends on — and how far there is to go.
board.mix_leader_find: Total electricity output of each source (bar length) for the slider year; the label shows the territory that produces the most and its share.
board.mix_leader_take: Oil dominates Pacific volume, but each source has its 'champion' — often a single territory carrying most of it.
board.mix_funnel_find: Total volume produced by each source across the WHOLE Pacific (funnel, largest to smallest), for the slider year.
board.mix_funnel_take: The funnel says it all: oil crushes the top, renewables share a narrow base.
board.mix_share_evo_find: Share of each source in the Pacific's total electricity generation (all territories combined), year by year (100% stacked bars). Hover = GWh produced.
board.mix_share_evo_take: Each source's share evolves over time: we follow a movement, we don't predict what's next.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two official datasets — renewables share (in %) and electricity production by source (in GWh) — with their units, nature and how to read a value.
board.read_take: An estimated share and reported volumes don't read alike: saying so is the condition of an honest reading.
board.coverage_find: For the renewable share: each cell says whether a value exists for that territory in that year. The UN estimates cover the period since 2000; gaps are shown, never filled.
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where each series starts and ends: this act's signature, like every other.

act5.coverage_title: Coverage of the renewable share: what the series contains — and what it doesn't

spotlight.r1v: UN Statistics Division (Energy Balances, 2024) · SDG indicator 7.2.1, EG_FEC_RNEW · via the Pacific Data Hub (DF_SDG)
spotlight.r2v: % of renewables in total final energy consumption · an ESTIMATED value · since 2000
spotlight.r3v: SPC · Power generation (DF_POWER_GEN) · national reporting · via the Pacific Data Hub
spotlight.r4v: GWh by source (hydro, solar PV, wind, geothermal, biofuels, biogas, fossil…) · “Total” grid connection used
spotlight.n1: Renewable share: a value estimated from the UN Statistics Division energy balances — it relates renewable energy consumed to total final consumption; it is a ratio, not a quantity.
spotlight.n2: Production by source: volumes reported at the national level, in GWh, broken down by source and grid-connection status. We keep only the total (on- + off-grid) to avoid double counting.
spotlight.n3: Two different natures: a percentage (share) and gigawatt-hours (volume) don't compare directly. A share can rise even as fossil volume also grows, if renewables grow faster.
spotlight.n4: Hydropower, dominant in several large countries, depends on rainfall: the renewable share can therefore swing year to year with no policy change.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years — the data is reformatted, never transformed.
spotlight.ex_text: Share: Fiji shows 50.06% renewables in 2000 and 26.67% in 2022 — hydro, sensitive to rainfall, makes the ratio swing. Production: in GWh, the per-source detail shows where solar and wind gain ground, year after year.
spotlight.link_label: See the renewables share (SDG 7.2.1, Pacific Data Hub)

act6.title: The nourishing land
act6.lead: How much does the land give back, crop by crop, animal by animal? FAOSTAT has measured it since 1961 — and the IMF's CALCI index tells, alongside, how land cover has shifted around its 2015 baseline. Two official datasets, two precise definitions, and many singular stories.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: We look at yield (kg harvested per hectare for crops, kg per producing animal for livestock), summarised per territory as the MEDIAN across all crops — a reading choice that gives each crop equal weight, different from the FAO total yield (sum of production ÷ sum of areas), which weights by area. The per-crop views show the data as is.

steps.v: Everything is in kg per hectare (or kg per animal): productivity, not total output nor cultivated area.
FAOSTAT holds both component crops and their aggregate totals (“Cereals, primary”, “Fruit, primary”…): aggregates are removed before analysis, following the producer's methodology.

The per-territory summary here is the median of per-crop yields (equal weight per crop). FAO's total-yield method divides total production by total harvested area. Both are legitimate — you just need to know which one you are reading.

steps.k: CALCI: an index, not an area
steps.v: The climate-altering land-cover index equals 100 for every country in 2015 (reference year). Computed by the IMF from FAO land-cover data; some values may be IMF-estimated.
Varieties, practices, inputs, markets, weather: a yield rarely moves for a single reason. No chart in this act attributes a change to climate — they locate and compare, which is already a lot.

guide.takeaway: Takeaway: per-crop median on one side, the FAO method on the other, the 2015-based CALCI index on the third — three definitions displayed, never conflated. And the official examples show yields improving.

act6.sub1_sub: Median yield per territory, across all crops — computed from real Pacific Data Hub data.
act6.heatmap_sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = yield
act6.change_sub: difference between the first and last available years
act6.explorer_lead: Pick a product to see its yield by territory and the most recent ranking.
act6.caveat_body: Agricultural yield does not depend on climate alone: farming practices, inputs, prices, planted area, pests and the estimation method weigh at least as much. Several series are also partly modelled (constant values) or have gaps. So we present this data as 'food systems under pressure', not as proof of a direct climate cause-and-effect.
act6.next: Coming next: livestock yield, then climate × yield cross-analysis.
act6.unavailable: Agricultural data momentarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub. Try again in a moment.
act6.trend_sub: One mini-chart per territory · each with its own scale (read the trend, not the level) · 1990–2024
act6.crop_rank_sub: Median yield per crop across the displayed territories (kg/ha) — moves with the timeline
act6.regional_sub: Median yield across all territories, year by year (kg/ha)
act6.diversity_sub: Number of distinct crops tracked per territory — higher means a more varied agricultural basket
act6.volatility_sub: Coefficient of variation of yields over time, by territory — higher means more exposed to shocks
act6.compare_sub: Median yield per territory between two chosen years — the bar links both values
act6.sub2_sub: Median yield per animal (kg/animal), territory by territory — from the same disaggregated dataset
act6.animal_rank_sub: Median yield per animal across the displayed territories (kg/animal)
act6.explorer_animal_lead: Pick an animal to see its yield by territory and the latest ranking.
act6.box_sub: spread across territories (Tukey box, log scale)
act6.cropmap_sub: yield by crop × territory, normalized per crop
act6.radar_sub: relative yield per crop (100 = best sub-region)

outro.title: A land that learns
outro.text: The official examples show yields improving — and definitions we now know how to read. Onward to what grows and lives uncultivated: the living world.

act6.thesis: Yields (kg/ha, kg/animal) and land cover (CALCI index, 2015 = 100): two official measures, each to be read for what it is — productivity on one side, a land-use index on the other. Yields rarely move for a single reason: here, we first learn to read them.

board.small_find: Each mini-chart a territory: the path of its median yield, current point marked.
board.small_take: Trajectories don't converge — which is the point of small multiples: each territory reads at home, with no mean flattening it.
board.regional_find: The region's median yield, year after year.
board.regional_take: A regional median gives the heading, never the detail: disparities read in the small multiples.
board.crops_find: Crops ranked by median yield for the selected year.
board.crops_take: A few crops dominate the ranking; the year selector shows whether that hierarchy holds over time.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's yield linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: Two real years compared: gains and setbacks coexist — the dumbbell measures a path, not its causes.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the yield at a glance.
board.heat_take: Good and bad years read as bands; the matrix separates the isolated accident from the settled trend.
board.map_find: The geography of median yield for the chosen year (log scale).
board.map_take: Fertile lands and poor soils are very unevenly spread across the Pacific.
board.explorer_find: Pick a crop to explore its yield, territory by territory.
board.explorer_take: Behind the aggregate, every crop tells its own story — this is where the data reads closest.
board.stability_find: Coefficient of variation of yield over time, per territory (low = steady).
board.stability_take: A low CV means a steady series — precious to know, without prejudging the reason.
board.race_find: Pick a product: the race ranks territories by yield, year after year.
board.race_take: The ranking reshuffles across products and years: run the race crop by crop — no yield is set in stone.
board.land_change_find: Change in the climate-altering land-cover index (CALCI) between 2015 (reference = 100) and the latest year, by territory.
board.land_change_take: Above 100: cover more altered than in 2015; below: less. Many territories stay close to their reference.
board.land_lines_find: Annual evolution of the CALCI index (2015 = 100) by territory, since 1992.
board.land_lines_take: Every curve meets 100 in 2015 by construction: the reading is about the path travelled before and after the reference.
board.land_slope_find: CALCI index at the first and last observed year, by territory (2015 = 100).
board.land_slope_take: The slope sums up three decades of land use, territory by territory — steep here, nearly flat there: the data distinguishes; ours is not to over-read.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two official datasets — FAOSTAT yields and the CALCI index — with the anti-double-counting rule, the yield formulas, and our median choice owned.
board.read_take: Saying how the aggregate is built — and how it differs from the producer's method — is the condition for reading it honestly.
board.coverage_find: For the chosen kind (crops or livestock): each cell says whether a median yield exists for that territory in that year. Gaps are shown, never filled.
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where each series ends: this act's signature, like every other.

act6.read_title: Two official datasets, one reading rule
act6.coverage_title: Yield coverage: what the series contains — and what it doesn't

spotlight.r1v: FAOSTAT · “Crops and livestock products” (QCL) · via the Pacific Data Hub, official dataset · CC BY 4.0 · upd. 2026-01-12
spotlight.r2v: Crops: Σ production ÷ Σ harvested area, in kg/ha · Livestock: Σ production ÷ producing animals, in kg/animal (FAO methodology)
spotlight.r3v: IMF · Climate Altering Land Cover Index (CALCI), computed from FAO land-cover data · via the Pacific Data Hub, official dataset · upd. 2026-01-11 · licence: see record
spotlight.r4v: Index, base 100 = 2015 for every country · only the aggregate CALCI indicator is kept (individual classes excluded)
spotlight.n1: FAOSTAT holds crops AND aggregate totals (“Cereals, primary”…): aggregates are removed before analysis to avoid double counting — the producer's rule, applied as is.
spotlight.n2: Our overview shows the MEDIAN of per-crop yields (equal weight per crop) — a reading choice different from FAO's total yield (area-weighted), owned and displayed. Per-crop views show the raw data.
spotlight.n3: CALCI: all computations are made by the IMF; some values may be IMF-estimated. 100 = the 2015 situation; the index reads as a gap to that reference, not as an area.
spotlight.n4: Two datasets, two natures: a yield (kg/ha) never compares to an index (base 100). Each measure reads against its own definition.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years (see Coverage) — and no causal attribution to climate.
spotlight.ex_text: Tonga, 2002: 6,114 kg/ha — 2022: 7,325 kg/ha, yields rising over twenty years. Kiribati, livestock: 1,460 kg/animal in 1962 — 2,508 in 2024. And on land: Tonga at 91.88 in 2006, i.e. 91.88% of the 2015 reference situation, back to 100 by 2022.

act7.title: The living world, through two indicators
act7.lead: Two official datasets for one subject: the Red List Index (from 0 to 1 — the higher, the less extinction looms) estimated by IUCN and BirdLife at the national scale, and the number of fisheries-management measures in force, catalogued by the FAO. One estimates a risk, the other counts decisions.
act7.redlist_sub: Red List Index (IUCN/BirdLife): 1 = pristine, 0 = all species extinct. It tracks aggregate extinction risk and declines across nearly every territory. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.fish_sub: Number of fisheries management measures in place (multilateral and bilateral agreements), cumulative per territory — steadily rising. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.unavailable: Living-world data temporarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub.
act7.rl_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of the index by territory and year ("trend" mode: each row on its own scale).
act7.rl_rank_sub: From least to most threatened. The lower the index, the higher the extinction risk.
act7.rl_compare_sub: Change in the index over three decades. ▼ = worsening (biodiversity loss).
act7.fish_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of measures in place by territory and year.
act7.fish_rank_sub: From most to least equipped. Larger Melanesia leads, smaller territories follow.
act7.fish_compare_sub: Growth in the number of measures since 2000. ▲ = stronger management.
act7.regional_rl_sub: Median Red List Index across visible territories, year by year. The slope falls: extinction risk worsens.
act7.regional_fish_sub: Median number of management measures in place across visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: governance strengthens.
act7.gauge_title: The health of life in one figure
act7.gauge_sub: median Red List Index of the selection (×100)
act7.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: fisheries management (X) vs Red List Index (Y)

story.rl_trend: The regional median Red List Index slips slowly downward: extinction risk is rising. The band shows the spread between territories.
story.fish_trend: Fisheries management measures pile up: an institutional response climbing, territory by territory.
story.gauge: The closer the index is to 100, the better life is preserved. Switch sub-region or territory to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing both indicators, we look for a link: does more fisheries management go hand in hand with a better Red List Index?

outro.title: What we protect, what we decide
outro.text: An index estimating risk, a counter of measures piling up: two official views of the living world, read for what they are. Now to where the sea meets the people: the territory.

act7.thesis: Red List Index (0–1, SDG 15.5.1) and fisheries-management measures (cumulative count, FAOLEX): two official indicators of different natures. One estimates, the other counts — read side by side, without confusing an estimate with a tally or inferring a cause.

board.trend_find: The regional median of the chosen measure, year after year, over the available coverage.
board.trend_take: The fair read happens over time and on the median: an index near 1 means low estimated risk, a rising count means measures accumulating.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: Each territory has its own trajectory: small multiples let each one read at home, with no mean flattening it.
board.race_find: The chosen measure replayed year after year, territory by territory.
board.race_take: For fisheries, the race only climbs — by design: the indicator is cumulative by construction (a measure stays counted until it is repealed).
board.change_find: For each territory, the first observed year's value joined to the last one's.
board.change_take: Two real years compared: the dumbbell measures a path, not a cause — the index and the count don't say why they move.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the state of nature at a glance.
board.heat_take: The matrix shows at a glance where the series exists and how the value spreads — light years, dark years.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year (green = better preserved).
board.map_take: The geography of the chosen measure at the last year: a snapshot — persistence reads in the trend and the matrix.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two official datasets — Red List Index and fisheries-management measures — with their natures, stated limits, and how to read a value.
board.read_take: An estimated index and a cumulative count don't read alike: saying so is the condition of an honest reading.
board.coverage_find: For the chosen measure: each cell says whether a value exists for that territory in that year. Crucial for fisheries, whose coverage is very uneven (43.1% missing globally).
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where each series starts and ends: this act's signature, like every other.

act7.coverage_title: Coverage of the chosen measure: what the series contains — and what it doesn't

spotlight.r1v: IUCN & BirdLife International (2020) · SDG 15.5.1, Red List Index · via the Pacific Data Hub (SPC2) · official dataset
spotlight.r2v: Index from 0 to 1 (1 = no extinction expected; lower = higher risk) · an ESTIMATED value, disaggregated to the national scale
spotlight.r3v: FAO · FAOLEX database (fisheries legislation), “Policies Dataset” · via the Pacific Data Hub · official dataset · CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 IGO · upd. 2025-09-03
spotlight.r4v: Number of management measures in force · CUMULATIVE count (+1 in the adoption year and every year after, unless repealed)
spotlight.n1: Red List: an estimate based on the extinction risk (IUCN categories) of all mammals, birds, amphibians, corals and cycads, derived from local and national data and weighted by each species's range share in the country. It is an estimated, nationally disaggregated value — not a direct field-by-field measurement.
spotlight.n2: Fisheries: the indicator is cumulative and monotonically increasing by construction (it counts active measures) — so a rise is not a single-year “event” but the accumulation of measures still in force.
spotlight.n3: Fisheries: the date used is that of the original text, failing which the last modification (a conservative estimate); coverage varies widely across countries (43.1% missing globally) — hence the Coverage tab.
spotlight.n4: Two indicators of different natures: you don't compare a 0–1 index to a count of measures, and placing them side by side demonstrates no cause and effect.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years — the data is reformatted, never transformed.
spotlight.ex_text: Red List: an index of 0.92 (Papua New Guinea, 1993) is close to 1 — low estimated extinction risk; 0.82 in 2024, lower — higher estimated risk. Fisheries: Tuvalu had 3 measures in force in 1976, and 43 by 2022.
spotlight.link_label: See the Red List Index (SDG 15.5.1, Pacific Data Hub)

act8.title: Reading the sky
act8.lead: Above the land, two gaps are measured — rainfall against its 1991–2020 normal (WMO standard), 2-m temperature against the 1971–2000 normal — and a third series tells who measures: the network of operational stations, recorded by the WMO since the 19th century.
act8.unavailable: Sky data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act8.credit: Sources: rainfall GPCP v2.3 (NOAA, CC0, 1991–2020 normal) · temperature NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 (NOAA/NCEI, CC0, 1971–2000 normal, 2 m) · WMO/OSCAR network (CC BY-SA 4.0). Dissemination: Pacific Data Hub (.Stat) — SPC, official datasets.
act8.rain_sub: Annual precipitation anomaly (mm) against the 1991–2020 normal. 0 = normal · negative = drier year · positive = wetter year.
act8.regional_rain_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The band shows how much islands diverge; 0 = the 1991–2020 normal.
act8.rain_hm_sub: Departure from the 1991–2020 normal, territory × year.
act8.rain_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.temp_sub: Land surface temperature anomaly (°C, at 2 m) against the 1971–2000 normal. 0 = normal · negative = cooler · positive = warmer.
act8.regional_temp_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: the land surface is warming; 0 = the 1971–2000 normal.
act8.temp_hm_sub: Departure from the 1971–2000 normal, territory × year.
act8.temp_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.meteo_sub: Number of operational weather stations, cumulative year by year (WMO/OSCAR). The larger the network, the better the Pacific measures its own climate.
act8.regional_meteo_sub: Total operational stations across visible territories, year by year. The line rises: observing capacity strengthens.
act8.meteo_hm_sub: Number of stations by territory and year.
act8.meteo_rank_sub: Number of stations at the last available year; reference = median of visible territories.
act8.meteo_cmp_sub: Number of stations from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.rain_guide_intro: GPCP v2.3 data (NOAA), 2.5° grid, monthly totals aggregated to an annual total per country. The anomaly is the departure from the 1991–2020 reference mean (WMO standard).
act8.rain_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1991–2020 normal. Above = wetter year, below = drier year.
act8.rain_guide_s2_v: The min–max band shows the spread across islands; each value also carries a standard error (uncertainty) computed from the year's monthly rainfall.
act8.rain_guide_s3_v: In millimetres: −20 means 20 mm below the normal over the year.
act8.rain_guide_takeaway: Example: Wallis & Futuna saw −20.5 in 1998 (a dry year) then +15 in 2017 (a wet year).
act8.temp_guide_intro: NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 data, 5° grid, monthly land surface anomalies (at 2 m) aggregated to an annual mean per country. Reference: 1971–2000.
act8.temp_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1971–2000 normal. Above = warmer than reference, below = cooler.
act8.temp_guide_s2_v: A standard error accompanies each value; early years (19th century) rest on statistical reconstruction, so they are more uncertain.
act8.temp_guide_s3_v: In degrees Celsius: +0.8 °C = land surface 0.8 °C above the normal.
act8.temp_guide_takeaway: Example: at Tokelau the anomaly moves from −0.94 °C in 1890 to +0.83 °C in 2024 — substantial warming.
act8.meteo_guide_intro: WMO data, OSCAR database. Only operational stations are counted; the indicator adds 1 per active year of each station, between opening and closure (or today).
act8.meteo_guide_s1_v: We rebuild each territory's total by summing station types (PDH also provides the breakdown by type).
act8.meteo_guide_s2_v: Conservative filtering (excluding 'Silent' or 'Unknown' stations) may underestimate the network but keeps the data robust.
act8.meteo_guide_s3_v: The higher the count, the stronger the capacity to observe the climate — this is the response to the disruptions.
act8.meteo_guide_takeaway: Example: in Samoa, 1 station in 1889, 4 stations in 2025.
act8.box_sub: range of anomalies, year by year
act8.share_warm_sub: share of territories with a positive anomaly (warmer than normal)
act8.share_wet_sub: share of territories with a positive anomaly (wetter than normal)
act8.gauge_sub: share with a positive temperature anomaly
act8.scatter_title: Do warming and rainfall move together?
act8.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: temperature anomaly (X) × precipitation anomaly (Y)

story.rain_trend: The regional median drifts from normal (line 0); the band shows the spread between dry and wet territories.
story.temp_trend: The temperature anomaly drifts above normal: warming reads directly against the 0 line.
story.box: Each box summarises a year: median, interquartile range and outliers across territories.
story.share_wet: The share of territories wetter than normal, year by year — is exposure becoming widespread?
story.share_warm: The share of territories warmer than normal climbs: warming becomes nearly universal.
story.gauge: In the latest year, the proportion of territories above the thermal normal. Switch sub-region to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing both anomalies, we look for a pattern: does warming go with more drought or more rain?

outro.title: A sky under watch
outro.text: Rainfall and temperatures read against their normals — and the network measuring them grows, station after station. Now to the extreme events: forty-seven seasons of cyclone tracks.

act8.thesis: Three official datasets for one sky: rainfall and temperature gaps to their respective normals — and the station network that makes knowing them possible in the first place.

board.trend_find: The regional anomaly (mean + min–max spread), year after year, against the normal specific to the chosen measure.
board.trend_take: The fair read happens over time: where the curve sits against its normal, decade by decade — not a single year's peak.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: Each territory has its own trajectory: small multiples let each one read at home, with no mean flattening them.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the deviation from normal (blue below, red above).
board.heat_take: The matrix tells at a glance what oscillates from what settles in — blue below the normal, red above.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first observed year's value joined to the last one's.
board.change_take: Two real years compared: the dumbbell measures a path, not a trend — context lives in the Trend view.
board.race_find: The cumulative count of operational stations, territory by territory, replayed year after year.
board.race_take: Station after station, the network grows: measuring your own sky is the first adaptation of all.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year.
board.map_take: The geography of the chosen measure, at the last year: a snapshot — persistence reads in the matrix and the trend.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the three official datasets — rain, temperature, network — with their three distinct references, and how to read a value.
board.read_take: Three measures, three normals: each value reads against its own reference, never against another's.
board.coverage_find: For the chosen measure: each cell says whether a value exists for that territory in that year. Gaps are shown as they are, never filled in.
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where each series ends: this act's signature, like every other.

spotlight.r1v: NOAA · GPCP v2.3 (Monthly CDR), 2.5° × 2.5° grid · via the Pacific Data Hub, official dataset · CC0 · upd. 2026-02-03
spotlight.r2v: mm (annual total − reference mean) · 1991–2020 normal, current WMO standard
spotlight.r3v: NOAA/NCEI · NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0, 5° × 5° grid · via the Pacific Data Hub, official dataset · CC0 · upd. 2026-01-12
spotlight.r4v: °C, at 2 m above the surface · gap to the 1971–2000 normal · spatial mean per country
spotlight.r5v: WMO · OSCAR database · via the Pacific Data Hub, official dataset · CC BY-SA 4.0 · upd. 2025-09-09
spotlight.r6v: Cumulative operational stations, +1 per year between establishment and closure (or the present) · total and by station type
spotlight.n1: Rain: annual totals summed from monthly data, anomaly = total − 1991–2020 mean; the standard error of the year's months serves as the uncertainty metric.
spotlight.n2: Temperature: per-country mean of a coarse grid refined geometrically — with no gain in real resolution; early periods rest on statistical reconstruction with higher uncertainty.
spotlight.n3: Network: only operational stations are counted (“Silent” and “Unknown” statuses excluded) — a conservative producer choice that may understate the total but guarantees robustness. Official example: Samoa, 1 station in 1889, 4 in 2025.
spotlight.n4: Three datasets, three references: a rain value never compares to a temperature value — each measure reads against its own normal.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years (see Coverage).
spotlight.ex_text: Wallis and Futuna, 1998: −20.5 mm — a drier year than the 1991–2020 normal. 2017: +15 mm — wetter. The sign gives the direction; the normal, the point of comparison.

act9.title: The economy, read closely
act9.lead: Two official datasets of different natures: visitor arrivals (counts, UN Tourism) and environmental taxation (% of GDP, IMF ↠ OECD). Each is exploited down to its detail — tourists and same-day visitors on one side, tax types on the other — without confusing a headcount with a share.
act9.unavailable: Economic data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act9.credit: Sources: tourism UN Tourism (CC-BY) · environmental taxation IMF ↠ OECD.
act9.tour_sub: Visitor arrivals (counts), source UN Tourism. An economic backbone of the islands — and among the first hit by cyclones, damaged reefs and global shocks.
act9.regional_tour_sub: Sum of arrivals across visible territories, year by year. The 2020–2021 crater reveals how fragile a heavily dependent model is.
act9.tour_rank_sub: Arrivals at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.tour_cmp_sub: Arrivals from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.power_sub: Electricity generation (GWh), source IMF/IRENA. Includes grid, off-grid and self-consumption by energy industries.
act9.regional_power_sub: Sum of generation across visible territories, year by year. Demand climbs: growing pressure on the energy mix.
act9.power_hm_sub: GWh by territory and year (row-normalised, log scale).
act9.power_rank_sub: Generation at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.power_cmp_sub: Generation from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.tax_sub: Environmental taxes as % of GDP (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources), source IMF/OECD. Very limited coverage: only a few countries report — that scarcity is the point.
act9.regional_tax_sub: Median of reporting territories, year by year. The line stays low: the fiscal climate lever is barely used.
act9.tax_sm_title: Each reporting territory, year by year
act9.tax_hm_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) by territory and year.
act9.tax_rank_sub: Peak environmental taxes (% of GDP) reached over the whole period; reference = median of peaks. Territories with no documented tax are omitted.
act9.tax_cmp_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) from the first to the last available year.
act9.tour_guide_intro: UN Tourism data (arrival statistics). A tourist = a visitor staying at least one night; the total also includes same-day excursionists.
act9.tour_guide_s1_v: Absolute counts: 200,000 = 200,000 arrivals in the year.
act9.tour_guide_s2_v: The sharp 2020–2021 drop (COVID) shows how these economies hang by a single thread.
act9.tour_guide_s3_v: Bleached reefs, eroded beaches, more frequent cyclones: the climate directly threatens tourism appeal.
act9.tour_guide_takeaway: Example: French Polynesia went from 201,000 arrivals (1997) to 261,400 (2022).
act9.power_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from IRENA. The total sums all technologies and grid-connection types per country-year.
act9.power_guide_s1_v: In gigawatt-hours (GWh): the energy produced over the year.
act9.power_guide_s2_v: Includes grid-connected, off-grid and electricity self-consumed by energy industries.
act9.power_guide_s3_v: Rising generation = rising demand, hence a transition challenge toward renewables (see Act 5).
act9.power_guide_takeaway: Example: Fiji rose from about 673 GWh (2000) to 1,285 GWh (2023).
act9.tax_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from the OECD. Only percentage-of-GDP observations are kept, broken down by tax type (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources).
act9.tax_guide_s1_v: As % of GDP: 0.89 means 0.89% of GDP comes from environmental taxes.
act9.tax_guide_s2_v: Extremely limited coverage (~44% missing; only a handful of countries). Few territories pull this lever.
act9.tax_guide_s3_v: The total is reliable only when all categories are documented for a country-year; we obtain it by summing the types.
act9.tax_guide_takeaway: Example: in Fiji, the weight of these taxes goes from 0.89% of GDP (2011) to 0.4% (2020).
act9.scatter_title: Do tourism and energy move together?
act9.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: arrivals (X) × electricity generation (Y)
act9.gauge_title: Is the fiscal lever being used?

story.tour_tree: Area shows the share: at a glance, which territories concentrate arrivals.
story.tour_pareto: The cumulative line climbs fast: a handful of destinations makes up most of regional tourism.
story.scatter: Crossing tourism and electricity reveals economic size: are the big destinations also the most energy-hungry?
story.gauge: Environmental taxation, as a share of GDP, stays modest: a lever barely pulled. Switch sub-region to compare.

outro.title: Two levers, read for what they are
outro.text: Tourism, a central resource to manage; green taxation, a still-rare lever taking hold. Two datasets decomposed without extrapolating. Time to bring it all together.

act9.thesis: Visitor arrivals (counts, UN Tourism) and environmental taxation (% of GDP, IMF ↠ OECD). Two official indicators read for what they are — a count and a ratio — and broken down: visitor levels, tax types.

board.trend_find: The regional total, year by year: a single line for the overall dynamic. Sharp drops mark shocks (e.g. the 2020 collapse).
board.trend_take: A single line for the regional dynamic: read the overall movement, without mistaking it for one territory's situation.
board.multiples_find: One mini-chart per territory: the line follows its arrivals year after year. Each card has its own scale — read the shape (the trend), not the level. The large figure is the latest known value, the ▲/▼ badge the change since the start. Click a card to enlarge it.
board.multiples_take: One mini-chart per territory: each reads at home, at its own scale — what the regional sum hides.
board.race_find: The territory ranking reshuffling year by year: bars overtake one another as one pulls ahead. Watch who climbs and who falls back.
board.race_take: The ranking reshuffles slowly: positions are settled, but relative movement stays visible year to year.
board.change_find: For each territory, its first known value (muted dot) linked to its latest (warm dot). Colour gives the direction (up / down), the bar length the size of the change. Sorted by latest value.
board.change_take: First known value linked to the last: a gap per territory, to read as a journey, not a cause.
board.heat_find: Territories (rows) × years (columns): colour shows the level of arrivals, from dark (low) to gold (high). Grey marks a year with no data. The busiest sits on top.
board.heat_take: The grid shows where and when data exists; grey bands are years with no data, never filled.
board.radar_find: Each sub-region's average profile, decade by decade: the larger the area, the higher the arrivals. Compare the shapes across Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia don't share a profile: three different scales, to compare with care.
board.map_find: The geography of the latest known year: each territory shaded by its level. Hover a territory for detail.
board.map_take: The geography of the last known year: a snapshot, to complete with the trend and coverage.
board.tax_compo_find: For reporting territories: the share of each tax type (energy, transport, pollution, resources) stacked year by year. You see what the effort is actually made of, not just its total.
board.tax_compo_take: Energy and transport most often carry the bulk; pollution and resources stay marginal. The composition tells a policy, not just an amount.
board.tour_compo_find: Where the detail exists: tourists (at least one overnight) and same-day visitors stacked. Their sum reconstructs the total arrivals.
board.tour_compo_take: The share of same-day visitors varies sharply with geography and connections: the same arrivals total can hide very different realities.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two official datasets — visitor arrivals (counts, UN Tourism) and environmental taxation (% of GDP, IMF ↠ OECD) — with units, natures, coverage and how to read a value.
board.read_take: An arrivals count and a share of GDP don't read alike: saying so is the condition of an honest reading.
board.coverage_find: Each cell says whether a value exists for that territory in that year. Taxation is very thinly covered (≈ 44% missing, a handful of reporters); gaps are shown, never filled.
board.coverage_take: Seeing exactly where each series starts and ends — all the more when coverage is thin, as for taxation.
board.tree_find: Each territory's share of arrivals, at its last known value: area shows weight.
board.tree_take: A handful of destinations capture most visitors; read the area, not a ranking.
board.pareto_find: Territories ranked by arrivals, with the cumulative share: you see how few destinations make up most of the total.
board.pareto_take: The cumulative curve rises fast: regional tourism rests on very few destinations.
board.donut_find: The average share of each tax type (energy, transport, pollution, resources) in the reporting territories' effort.
board.donut_take: Energy and transport almost always dominate; pollution and resources stay at the margin.

spotlight.r3v: IMF (climate dashboard) ↠ OECD · environmental taxes (ENVTX) · via the Pacific Data Hub (DF_ENV_TAXES)
spotlight.r4v: % of GDP · split: energy · transport · pollution · resources (+ total) · 5 reporting SPC countries, ≈ 44% missing
spotlight.n1: Tourism: a tourist spends at least one night on site; same-day visitors are counted in the total. Values are absolute counts — a headcount, not a rate.
spotlight.n2: Taxation: only observations in percent of GDP are kept. The total is computed only when all four categories (energy, transport, pollution, resources) are documented for a country-year.
spotlight.n3: Two different natures: an arrivals count and a % of GDP don't compare directly. Read side by side, never one in the other's unit.
spotlight.n4: Very uneven coverage: tourism is broadly reported; environmental taxation only for 5 SPC member countries (≈ 44% missing overall).
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing, no correction, no filling of missing years — the data is reformatted, never transformed. UN Tourism note: new unified questionnaire, processing in transition, completeness still improving.
