act10.title: Water and health
act10.lead: Closest to the inhabitants: safely managed drinking water (share of population, in %) and tuberculosis incidence (cases per 100,000). Two official UN indicators, estimated since 2000 — and two opposite reading directions: for water, higher is better; for tuberculosis, lower is better.
act10.unavailable: Health data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act10.credit: Sources: water WHO/UNICEF (JMP) · tuberculosis WHO (Global TB Report 2024). Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act10.water_sub: Share of the population using safely managed drinking water services (%), source WHO/UNICEF. A fragile resource: saltwater intrusion into atoll aquifers, droughts, networks destroyed by cyclones.
act10.regional_water_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, higher is better: the goal is 100%.
act10.water_hm_sub: % of population by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means higher access.
act10.water_rank_sub: % at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.water_cmp_sub: Access to safe water from the first to the last available year. Rising = progress.
act10.tb_sub: Tuberculosis incidence per 100,000 people, source WHO. A marker of vulnerability (poverty, crowding, nutrition): climate acts mostly indirectly, through displacement and shocks.
act10.regional_tb_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, lower is better: a decline is progress.
act10.tb_hm_sub: Cases / 100,000 by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means a heavier burden.
act10.tb_rank_sub: Cases / 100,000 at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.tb_cmp_sub: Incidence from the first to the last available year. Falling = improvement.
act10.water_guide_title: How to read — safe drinking water
act10.water_guide_intro: WHO/UNICEF data (Joint Monitoring Programme, JMP). A 'safely managed' service = water accessible at home, available when needed and free from contamination.
act10.water_guide_s1_v: As % of population: 95 means 95% of people have safe water.
act10.water_guide_s2_v: Higher is better. A rising line = progress; a falling one (as in Solomon Islands) = a retreat to watch.
act10.water_guide_s3_v: On atolls, rising seas salinise aquifers; droughts and cyclones cut access. Safe water is on the front line.
act10.water_guide_takeaway: Example: Papua New Guinea went from about 32% (2000) to 50% (2022), while Solomon Islands fell from 79% to 67%.
act10.tb_guide_intro: WHO data (Global Tuberculosis Report 2024). Incidence measures estimated new cases each year, relative to population.
act10.tb_guide_s1_v: As cases per 100,000 people: 100 = 100 new cases a year per 100,000 people.
act10.tb_guide_s2_v: Lower is better. A decline is progress. On the maps, a darker cell = a heavier burden.
act10.tb_guide_s3_v: For very small populations (Niue, Tokelau…), a single case sends the rate soaring: sharp swings there must be taken with caution. The climate link is indirect.
act10.tb_guide_takeaway: Example: Vanuatu cut its incidence from 110 (2000) to 41 (2023); conversely, it climbs in Kiribati (~355 → 533).
act10.box_sub: median, interquartile range and outliers, by year
act10.scatter_title: Does water shield from the burden?
act10.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: drinking-water access (X) × tuberculosis (Y)

story.water_band: The line tracks the regional median; the p10–p90 band shows the gap between best- and least-served territories.
story.tb_band: The regional median incidence; the p10–p90 band reveals the wide gaps in burden between territories.
story.box: Each box summarises a year: median, interquartile range and outliers across territories.
story.gauge: The median drinking-water access of the selection. The fuller the arc, the better. Switch sub-region to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing water access and tuberculosis, we look for the expected link: does better sanitation go with a lower burden?

outro.title: Closest to the inhabitants
outro.text: Water improving, tuberculosis receding: two estimated indicators, each read in its own direction. Now to what hazards leave behind — the impact of disasters.

act10.thesis: Safely managed drinking water (%, WHO/UNICEF) and tuberculosis incidence (cases/100,000, WHO): two UN indicators, model-estimated. Opposite polarities — colour and the before→after direction adjust to the chosen measure so they never mislead.

board.trend_find: The regional median of the chosen measure, year after year, since 2000.
board.trend_take: The fair read happens over time and on the median: for water, a rising curve is progress; for tuberculosis, it's a falling curve.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: Each territory has its own trajectory: small multiples let each one read at home, with no mean flattening it.
board.race_find: The territories' race on the indicator, year after year.
board.race_take: The ranking reshuffles over the years — remember the measure's direction: high = better for water, high = worse for tuberculosis.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first observed year's value joined to the last one's.
board.change_take: Two real years compared: the dumbbell measures a path. The legend gives the direction — progress or setback depending on the measure.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the level at a glance.
board.heat_take: The matrix shows at a glance where the series exists and how the value spreads over time.
board.radar_find: The average profile of each sub-region, decade by decade.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia have distinct profiles: the radar compares them without ranking them.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year.
board.map_take: The geography of the chosen measure at the last year: a snapshot — persistence reads in the trend and the matrix.
board.read_find: The ID cards of the two UN indicators — safely managed drinking water and tuberculosis incidence — with their precise definitions, their estimated nature, and how to read a value.
board.read_take: Two estimated indicators, two opposite polarities: saying so plainly is the condition of a fair reading.
board.coverage_find: For the chosen measure: each cell says whether a value exists for that territory in that year. The UN estimates cover water and TB since 2000.
board.coverage_take: Knowing exactly where each series starts and ends: this act's signature, like every other.

act10.read_title: Two UN indicators, two reading directions
act10.coverage_title: Coverage of the chosen measure: what the series contains — and what it doesn't

spotlight.r1v: WHO & UNICEF · Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) · SDG 6.1.1, SH_H2O_SAFE · via the Pacific Data Hub · upd. 2024-09-27
spotlight.r2v: % of population using a safely managed drinking water service: improved source, accessible on premises, available when needed, free from contamination · higher = better
spotlight.r3v: WHO · SDG 3.3.2, SH_TBS_INCD · via the Pacific Data Hub · upd. 2026-03-27
spotlight.r4v: Estimated number of new and relapse cases per year, per 100,000 population · lower = better
spotlight.n1: Water: an ESTIMATED value via linear regression on data available since 2000 (including years without a reading), from censuses, household surveys and administrative data; “safely managed” = the minimum of three criteria (accessibility, availability, quality).
spotlight.n2: Tuberculosis: an ESTIMATED value (notifications adjusted for under-reporting and under-diagnosis, prevalence surveys, dynamic models for some countries after 2020); WHO provides uncertainty bounds around each estimate.
spotlight.n3: OPPOSITE polarities: for water, a high value is favourable; for tuberculosis, a high value is unfavourable. Map colours and the before→after direction adjust to the displayed measure.
spotlight.n4: Both indicators rely on UN Population Division estimates — figures may differ from national sources.
spotlight.n5: What we do not do: no smoothing or filling on our side. What the producers do (modelling, imputation), we relay as is and flag here, without hiding it.
spotlight.ex_text: Water: Papua New Guinea, from about 32% (2000) to 50% (2022) — access improving. Tuberculosis: Vanuatu, from 110 cases/100,000 (2000) to 41 (2023) — incidence falling. Two measures, two directions, the same progress.
spotlight.link_label: See safely managed drinking water (SDG 6.1.1, UN metadata)

act11.title: Guilty of almost nothing, exposed to almost everything
act11.lead: Ten acts, one thread. Here we cross everything we've seen: what these islands emit, and what they bear. Responsibility on one side, vulnerability on the other — and the gap that lays bare the Pacific's climate injustice.
act11.unavailable: Synthesis data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act11.credit: Sources: Pacific Data Hub (SPC) — GHG/capita, sea level & SST (IMF, NOAA), precipitation (GPCP), drinking water (WHO/UNICEF), tuberculosis (WHO), Red List (IUCN).
act11.credit_partial: Some indicators were unavailable at load time: the index is computed on those that responded.
act11.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory · X: GHG per capita · Y: vulnerability index
act11.rank_sub: composite index, most to least exposed
act11.profile_sub: Index broken down by dimension (0–100, 100 = the Pacific's worst on that dimension). Pick 'All' to return to the ranking.
act11.guide_title: How to read — the vulnerability index
act11.guide_intro: We bring together six signals seen in earlier acts — sea level, sea-temperature anomaly, rainfall deviation, drinking-water access, tuberculosis, Red List Index — for each territory, at their last known value.
act11.guide_s1_v: Each indicator is rescaled to 0–100 (the Pacific's worst = 100), with the right polarity: water and biodiversity are inverted (low = worse), rainfall counted as absolute deviation. The index is the equal-weight average of available dimensions.
act11.guide_s2_v: Right of the scatter = emits more per head; up = more exposed. The top-left quadrant gathers those who weigh little on the climate yet suffer it most — the heart of the injustice.
act11.guide_s3_v: This is not an official index: adjustable weights, rank normalisation within the Pacific only, volatile small populations, and a territory is scored only if it has enough dimensions. Read it as a construction, not a verdict.
act11.guide_takeaway: The overall pattern fits in one line: the smallest emitters are rarely the least exposed — often the opposite.
act11.stat_inj_sub: territories below the regional emissions median AND above the vulnerability median
act11.cross_sub: one side emits, the other bears it — let's confront responsibility and vulnerability
act11.bubble_sub: X: GHG/capita · Y: vulnerability · bubble size: exposure to sea-level rise
act11.slope_sub: each line links responsibility (left) to vulnerability (right), 0 to 100
act11.donut_sub: each sub-region's share of the Pacific's cumulative vulnerability
act11.treemap_sub: each tile's area is proportional to the vulnerability index
act11.pareto_sub: GHG per capita, highest to lowest, with cumulative share
act11.fingerprint_sub: each territory has its own vulnerability signature
act11.matrix_sub: territories × six dimensions · green = low, red = high (0–100)
act11.radar_sub: average vulnerability per dimension — Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia
act11.polar_sub: the six dimensions fanned out as sectors (0–100)
act11.radial_sub: vulnerability index of the six leading territories
act11.gauge_sub: median composite index of the current selection

story.agri_x: Each tile is a product. Its area shows how many territories grow it — the regional food staples fill the space, niche production shrinks to a corner. Green is plant, coral is animal; the brighter the hue, the higher the median yield. At a glance you read what Pacific food security rests on — and how much it hangs on a handful of crops.
story.agri_m: Method: one tile per product. Size ∝ ubiquity (number of territories reporting it — a unit-free measure, honest to aggregate); colour = type (crop / livestock); intensity = median yield (latest year), normalised by rank WITHIN its category, since kg/ha are never compared to kg/animal. Source: agricultural production, Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
story.swarm_text: An average hides everything: it says “the Pacific is doing fine” even if a few territories suffer enormously. Here, each circle is a territory, placed on the vulnerability scale. You see at once the pack — where most cluster — and the few that break away, downward or upward. It's the picture single numbers erase.
story.swarm_m: Method: each circle is a territory, placed on the composite index axis (0–100). No averaging: you see the real distribution — the pack and the outliers. Same scored territories as the other views.
story.bubble_title: And how much coast at stake
story.bubble_text: The same paradox, but each bubble is sized by the extent of its coastline. Left, the low emitters; top, the most vulnerable. The large bubbles in the shaded quadrant say it all: not abstract points but kilometres of inhabited coast, exposed by territories that emitted almost nothing.
story.bubble_m: Method: X = per-capita GHG emissions (t CO₂e), Y = composite vulnerability index (0–100), size ∝ coastline extent (number of tracked coast segments, an approximate proxy). Shaded quadrant = low emissions and high vulnerability. Colour by sub-region. Sources: Pacific Data Hub (SPC); coastline from Digital Earth Pacific.
story.mix_hint: Hover a source to follow it through the flow
story.top_m: Method: the 6 territories with the highest composite index (0–100), shown as rings to stay legible. This is not the full list: it is the top of the ranking. The index combines 6 stresses at equal weight, normalised by rank within the Pacific.
story.mix_x: Stacked area of the Pacific's electricity mix over the years, source by source: fossil fuels at the bottom (oil, diesel, coal, gas…), renewables on top (hydro, solar, wind, geothermal, biomass). At a glance: a still-heavy reliance on fossil fuels — and a renewable share gaining ground, too slowly.
story.mix_m: Method: generation by source (GWh), grid-connected ‘Total’ only, aggregated across all Pacific territories and summed per year. Fossil sources in coral, renewables in green. Source: IRENA via Pacific Data Hub (IMF / climate change).
story.scatter: Read left to right: the further left, the fewer emissions per capita. The higher up, the more vulnerable. The vertical line marks the regional emissions median, the horizontal one the median vulnerability. The top-left corner — few emissions, high exposure — gathers the territories most exposed relative to their responsibility.
story.bubble: Same plane, but bubble size now adds exposure to sea-level rise. The big bubbles clustered on the left say it plainly: those hit hardest by the sea are not those who loaded the atmosphere.
story.slope: Each line starts at a territory's responsibility (left) and reaches its vulnerability (right). An upward slope (coral) = few emissions but high exposure. The thicket of rising slopes sums up the whole act.
story.donut: Cumulative vulnerability isn't shared evenly: one sub-region holds a markedly larger slice. The ring shows at a glance where the risk weighs most.
story.treemap: The bigger the tile, the more vulnerable the territory. The hierarchy jumps out without reading a single figure.
story.pareto: Bars rank the emitters, the line accumulates their share. Its fast rise shows responsibility concentrates on a handful of territories.
story.matrix: One row per territory, one column per dimension. Red bands reveal the pressure points: some pile up fragilities, others have just one or two critical ones.
story.radar: Each polygon's shape tells where a sub-region hurts most: the sea here, water or health there. Three geographies of risk, overlaid.
story.polar: The six dimensions open out like a fan. The longer a sector, the more that threat weighs in the chosen profile — a single territory, or the selection's average.
story.radial: The rings compare the leading territories: their composite index, side by side, to see the gap among the most exposed.
story.gauge: The median vulnerability level of the selection. Switch sub-region or territory and watch the needle move.
story.rank: The full vulnerability ranking. In territory mode it becomes the breakdown of that territory's six dimensions, with 50 = the average.
story.gap: Here is the debt, plainly: for each territory, subtract responsibility (its emissions) from vulnerability (what it bears). The long coral bars to the right are the territories paying the heaviest price for a disruption they didn't cause. The cyan bars on the left are the reverse — rare in the Pacific.
story.mirror: On the left what a territory emits, on the right what it bears, back to back. When the right wing (coral) dwarfs the left (cyan), the injustice is obvious: much exposure for very little responsibility.
story.sloperank: The same reversal, by rank: left, the emitters' ranking (1 = most responsible); right, the most vulnerable. The lines that plunge — high on the right, low on the left — are the most unjustly exposed territories.
story.voyage_text: Before the verdict, let's retrace the path — each act, a piece of the Pacific's climate puzzle.
story.resp_title: Modest emitters, on the front line
story.resp_text: Within the Pacific, CO₂ emissions per capita already vary widely from one territory to another. The regional median stays low — and it is often the territories that emit the least which bear the brunt of rising seas, cyclones and erosion.
story.ocean_text: Decade after decade, sea level rises across the three sub-regions. For low-lying atolls, every millimetre counts.
story.atlas_text: By crossing several stresses — sea, heat, rainfall, water, health, life — each territory gets a score relative to the others in the Pacific (percentile rank, 0–100). A high score says “among the most exposed in the region”, not an absolute value.
story.radar_text: Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia don't carry the same vulnerabilities — but none is spared.
story.matrix_text: Territories in rows, stresses in columns. Red accumulates: it's not one isolated risk, it's their sum.
story.paradox_title: Pollute the least, suffer the most
story.paradox_text: On the x-axis, responsibility (emissions per capita); on the y-axis, composite vulnerability. Each point is a Pacific territory: read against the regional medians, not an external benchmark.
story.focus_hint: Click a territory to highlight it.
story.recap_a2: Ocean — sea level and heat are rising.
story.recap_a3: Territory — populations concentrated on the coasts.
story.recap_a7: Living world — biodiversity and fisheries in retreat.
story.recap_a9: Economy — tourism and energy, exposed wealth.
story.recap_a10: Health — water and disease, persistent fragilities.
story.recap_a11: Synthesis — it all converges: the climate debt.
story.studio_text: By default the composite index gives each dimension the same weight. But no weighting is neutral. Adjust the weight of each stress — water, health, sea, life — and watch the ranking recompose. It's a construction, not a truth.
story.studio_hint: Drag the sliders: 0 removes the dimension, 2 doubles it.
story.reversal_text: This is the heart of the whole story in a single visual. On the left, territories ranked by their emissions; on the right, by their vulnerability. If the world were fair, the two columns would look alike. They flip: the lines rising diagonally are territories that emit little and absorb much. Responsibility on one side, the bill on the other.
story.top_title: Who is on the front line
story.top_text: The territories where the most stresses pile up at once. Not a trophy ranking, but a marker: it shows where, at the Pacific scale, attention concentrates. A high score is not a sentence — it's a signal to look closer.
story.limits_1: Coverage is uneven: some territories are scored on only two or three dimensions, others on all. A missing score is not a zero.
story.limits_2: The indices are relative to the Pacific: a high score says “among the most exposed in the region”, never an absolute value or a comparison outside the zone.
story.limits_3: Several series rely on values estimated by their producers, and small populations make some measures volatile from year to year.
story.limits_4: The index does not measure adaptive capacity, funding or current policies — it describes exposure, not fate.
story.context_text: Vulnerability is only one side of the story. The earlier acts also showed levers: electricity decarbonising, taxation starting to price carbon, an economy seeking its balance. This ranking is a reminder that behind exposure there is also room to act — uneven, but real.
story.ctx_renew_x: Vulnerability is only one side of the story. Here is the renewable share of final energy consumption (in %), territory by territory, at its last known value. The longer the bar, the more of the energy consumed comes from renewable sources: a lever of adaptation already at work, to uneven degrees.
story.ctx_envtax_x: Vulnerability is only one side of the story. Here is the weight of environmental taxes (as % of GDP), territory by territory, at its last known value. Coverage is limited: only a few territories report — but where the lever exists, it can be measured.
story.ctx_tour_x: Vulnerability is only one side of the story. These figures are visitor arrivals (in counts, not %), territory by territory, at their last known value — for example ~928,000 arrivals for Fiji. An economic backbone of the islands, tourism is also exposed to damaged reefs and cyclones: a resource to manage as much as to defend.
story.crops_x: Beyond exposure, there is what territories produce. Here is the median crop yield (in kg per hectare, all crops combined), territory by territory, at its last known value. The longer the bar, the more the land yields — an asset for food self-reliance against shocks.
story.stock_x: On the livestock side, here is the median yield per animal (in kg per producing animal), territory by territory, at its last known value. Another facet of food security, completing crops — each territory at its own scale.
story.envtax2_t: The fiscal lever, where it exists
story.envtax2_x: Here is the weight of environmental taxes (as % of GDP), territory by territory, at its last known value. Coverage is deliberately partial: only a few territories report. That scarcity is itself information about a lever still barely used.
story.power_x: Here is electricity generation (in GWh), territory by territory, at its last known value. The longer the bar, the more the territory produces — a measure of energy scale, and the stakes of a transition toward renewables (Act 5).
story.crops_m: Method: for each territory we take the median yield of all its crops (kg/ha) at the latest available year, then rank the top 12. Source: FAOSTAT via the Pacific Data Hub. Territories with no reported yield are not shown.
story.stock_m: Method: median yield per producing animal (kg/animal) at the latest available year, ranked on the top 12. Source: FAOSTAT via the Pacific Data Hub. A territory with no reported livestock data is not shown.
story.envtax2_m: Method: total environmental taxes as % of GDP, latest reported year, ranked descending. Source: IMF ↠ OECD via the Pacific Data Hub. Only the few reporting territories appear — an absence is not a zero, it is a non-report.
story.power_m: Method: electricity generation in GWh, latest available year, ranked descending. Source: IMF/IRENA via the Pacific Data Hub. A territory with no reported generation is not shown.
story.ctx_renew_m: Method: renewable share of final energy consumption (%), latest available year, top 12. Source: UN (SDG 7.2.1) via the Pacific Data Hub.
story.ctx_envtax_m: Method: total environmental taxes as % of GDP, latest reported year, top 12. Source: IMF ↠ OECD via the Pacific Data Hub. Partial coverage: only reporting territories appear.
story.ctx_tour_m: Method: visitor arrivals in counts (not %), latest available year, top 12. Source: UN Tourism via the Pacific Data Hub.
story.resp_m: Method: median per-capita GHG emissions (t CO₂e/cap.) across scored territories, at the latest year. The green bar is the Pacific median, the coral one the highest per-capita emitter. Source: World Bank via the Pacific Data Hub.
story.ocean_m: Method: median sea level per sub-region (Melanesia / Polynesia / Micronesia), year by year. You read a relative trajectory, not an absolute height. Source: Pacific Data Hub sea level.
story.atlas_m: Method: bubble size = composite vulnerability index (0–100), colour likewise. The index is the equal-weight average of 6 stresses (sea, heat, rainfall, water, tuberculosis, biodiversity), each normalised by percentile rank within the Pacific. A territory appears only if it has at least 2 of the 6 dimensions — hence the gaps.
story.radar_m: Method: for each sub-region, the average of its territories' normalised scores (0–100) on each of the 6 dimensions. The larger the area, the more exposed the sub-region on that axis. Scale relative to the Pacific.
story.matrix_m: Method: each cell is a territory's normalised score on one dimension (0 = the most spared in the Pacific, 100 = the most exposed). Rows are sorted by descending composite index. An empty cell = dimension unavailable for that territory.
story.paradox_m: Method: X axis = per-capita GHG emissions (responsibility), Y axis = composite vulnerability index (0–100). The median lines split territories in two. Each dot is a scored territory; reading relative to the Pacific.
story.reversal_m: Method: on the left, the per-capita emissions rank (1 = biggest emitter); on the right, the vulnerability rank. Both ranks are computed on the same scored territories. A rising line = few emissions but high exposure.
story.studio_m: Method: you adjust each dimension's weight (0 = removed, 1 = normal, 2 = doubled); the composite index is recomputed live as the weighted average of available dimensions, then re-normalised. No weighting is neutral — that is the point of this view.
story.parallel_text: Each line is a territory crossing the six climate stresses, lowest to highest. Follow one: you instantly see whether the island is hit on every front at once — the line stays high from axis to axis — or only on one or two. Coral lines pile on the stresses; cyan ones fare better. When several lines rise and fall together, two stresses are moving in step.
story.parallel_m: Method: each indicator is normalised by percentile rank (0–100) within the Pacific, then plotted on its own axis. Colour reflects the territory's average stress across available axes; a missing segment marks unavailable data. Source: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
story.corr_text: Each cell crosses two stresses and measures whether they move together across the Pacific. In coral, two pressures that rise together: territories hit hard by one are often hit by the other. In cyan, two pressures that pull apart. The bolder the colour, the clearer the link. Across only about fifteen territories it's an indicative read — but it reveals the hidden structure of vulnerability.
story.corr_m: Method: Pearson correlation between percentile ranks (≈ Spearman), for each pair of indicators, over territories where both are available (n shown on hover). Diagonal neutralised. Colour runs from cyan (−1) through neutral to coral (+1). Small sample: read it as a signal, not a proof. Source: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).

outro.title: A reading, not a verdict
outro.text: None of this is set in stone. This index invents no data: it gathers official measurements, puts them on one scale, and lets you adjust what matters most. It doesn't rule — it shows where to act first, and opens the conversation rather than closing it.

act11.method: Method: latest value per indicator, normalised 0–100 (min-max), then averaged with equal weights. Indicative index, not official.
act11.gap_sub: vulnerability − responsibility, per territory · right = bears more than it owes
act11.mirror_title: Emits on one side, bears it on the other
act11.thesis: Eleven acts for one idea, with a note of hope: Pacific territories emit very little CO₂ per capita and absorb a great deal — yet keep real room to act. This final act gathers the evidence into one clear reading, relative to the Pacific, that you can explore and weight yourself.

board.paradox_take: The injustice is stark: the Pacific emits very little, yet ranks among the most vulnerable.
board.vuln_find: Composite index (0–100): normalized average of the six climate stresses, per territory.
board.vuln_take: The most exposed stack sea-level rise, heat, water and health strain.
board.matrix_find: Each row a territory, each column a stress: green = spared, red = under pressure.
board.matrix_take: No single vulnerability: it's the accumulation of stresses that makes the danger.
board.radar_find: The average profile of each sub-region across the six vulnerability dimensions.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia carry different but very real fragilities.
board.resp_find: GHG emissions per capita, ranked: each one's share of responsibility.
board.resp_take: Within the Pacific, even the biggest emitters stay at modest levels per capita.

calc.context: Method: latest known value per territory for the displayed indicator (renewable share in %, taxation in % of GDP, or arrivals in counts). Descending ranking, up to 12 territories. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
calc.crops: Method: for each territory, the median of all its crop yields (kg/ha) at the latest known year — FAO aggregates excluded. FAOSTAT data via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.stock: Method: for each territory, the median of livestock yields (kg per producing animal) at the latest known year. FAOSTAT data via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.envtax: Method: total environmental taxes as % of GDP, latest known value per territory. Partial coverage (few reporters). IMF ↠ OECD source via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.power: Method: electricity generation in GWh, latest known value per territory. IMF/IRENA source via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.resp: Method: GHG emissions per capita (t CO₂-eq), latest year. The green marker is the Pacific median — the value splitting territories in two halves. World Bank source via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.ocean: Method: for each sub-region, the median sea level of its territories, year by year. Relative level (gap to reference). Copernicus/NOAA source via Pacific Data Hub.
calc.atlas: Method: composite index 0–100 per territory — equal-weight average of the six climate stresses, normalised by percentile rank within the Pacific. Size = vulnerability, colour = same index.
calc.radar: Method: for each sub-region, the mean of its territories' normalised scores (0–100) on each of the six dimensions. The wider the area, the more exposed the sub-region on that dimension.
calc.matrix: Method: each cell = a territory's (row) normalised 0–100 score on a stress (column), by percentile rank within the Pacific. Green = low, red = high.
calc.paradox: Method: X = emissions per capita (t CO₂-eq), Y = composite vulnerability index (0–100). The lines mark the regional medians of both axes. Each dot is a Pacific territory.
calc.reversal: Method: left, emissions-per-capita rank (0–100); right, vulnerability rank (0–100), both by percentile rank within the Pacific. A rising slope = few emissions, high exposure.
calc.swarm: Method: each circle = a territory, placed on the composite vulnerability axis (0–100). No aggregation: you see the real distribution, the pack and the outliers.
calc.top: Method: composite vulnerability index (0–100) per territory, ranked most to least exposed. All scored territories are shown, with their exact score.
calc.studio: Method: the index recomputes live the weighted average of the six stresses according to the weights you set (0 = removed, 1 = normal, 2 = doubled). Rank normalisation within the Pacific.

about.title: Datamoana, and the hand behind the data
about.lead: An independent data-visualisation project built for the Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026: making the Pacific's climate data legible, at human scale.

author.name: Stoyann Velten
author.role: Developer & IT support — Nouméa, New Caledonia
author.body:
I live and work in Nouméa, New Caledonia, as a developer and IT support technician for one of the territory's largest hardware and building-supplies groups. Datamoana is a personal project — one I designed and built on my own, outside of working hours.

I took it on because the Pacific is home. The data we work with here is anything but abstract: it's the texture of daily life — the sea eating into our coastlines, the cyclones, the water we rely on, the state of the reefs. Yet it often stays scattered and technical, hard to reach for the very people it concerns most.

What I set out to do with Datamoana is simple: to make it clear and tangible, without ever bending the scales or overstating the case, so that anyone can grasp at a glance what our islands are truly living through.
author.note: Solo project · React, ECharts & Mapbox GL · Pacific Data Hub (SPC) data

data.title: Open, verifiable sources
data.lead: All of Datamoana's data comes from open, public sources, aggregated by the Pacific Community's (SPC) Pacific Data Hub .Stat. It matches the challenge's official list: each act shows its source, unit and method.
data.platform_desc: Unified access point (SDMX API) to the Pacific's regional statistics.
data.note: Technical access via the SDMX API: stats-sdmx-disseminate.pacificdata.org
data.integrity: Data transparency — All visualisations rely on real data fetched live from the Pacific Data Hub and official sources. When data is unavailable, the app shows “data unavailable” rather than estimated values: nothing is fabricated or hard-coded. The only documented exception — the coastline (Digital Earth Pacific — Landsat Coastlines, CC BY-NC 4.0), a large geospatial dataset provided as a precomputed extract; its per-territory figures (retreat, growth, spread) are an approximate aggregation, obtained by attaching each coastline segment to the nearest territory. Territory coordinates are used only to place points on the map.
data.coastline_dl_meta: Digital Earth Pacific — Landsat Coastlines · CC BY-NC 4.0

challenge.title: Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026
challenge.body: Datamoana is my entry to the 2026 Pacific Dataviz Challenge, whose theme is climate change in the Pacific. Twelve acts, 23 official datasets crossed, one story.

story.arc_text: Each arc links a territory from its responsibility — its rank in per-capita emissions — to its vulnerability. When an arc climbs to the right — few emissions, high exposure — it turns coral. The tangle of these coral arcs, all leaning the same way, captures the Pacific's climate injustice at a glance: those who weigh the least bear the most.
story.arc_m: Method: responsibility is the percentile rank of per-capita GHG emissions within the Pacific (0 = lowest, 100 = highest); vulnerability is the synthesis composite index (0–100). Two different scales mapped to the same rank to stay comparable. Source: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
story.renew2_title: The effort: footprint vs clean energy
story.renew2_text: Here we set footprint — per-capita emissions, horizontal — against effort — share of renewable energy, vertical. Top-left, territories combining a low footprint with largely renewable power are leading the way; bottom-right, those where the transition still lies ahead.
story.renew2_m: Method: per-capita GHG emissions (latest year) on X; share of renewables in final energy consumption (%) on Y. One point per territory, grouped by sub-region. Source: SDG 7 and climate change, Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
story.title: When a number changes a decision
story.lead: I don't build charts for decoration. I look for the moment a single number changes a decision — when a pattern invisible to the eye becomes a concrete action. One story sums up that conviction better than any pitch.
story.context: In 2004, as Hurricane Frances bore down on Florida, Walmart's analysts dug through years of checkout data to anticipate demand. The goal: know what to stock before the storm made landfall.
story.found_1: Strawberry Pop-Tarts sales jumped to about 7 times normal right before a hurricane.
story.found_2: The top-selling item ahead of a storm wasn't water or batteries — it was beer.
story.found_3: Counter-intuitive correlations that no amount of field experience would have guessed.
story.why_1: Pop-Tarts keep for a long time.
story.why_2: They need no cooking and no refrigeration.
story.why_3: They stay edible even during a power outage.
story.why_4: Beer, meanwhile, gets bought ahead of several days stuck at home.
story.outcome: The outcome: Walmart began pre-positioning pallets of Pop-Tarts and beer in stores along storm tracks — a logistics decision born from a simple cross-reading of data.
story.legend_lead: Because it packs, into one anecdote, everything I practise day to day in Business Intelligence and Power BI:
story.concept_1_d: Surfacing hidden patterns in the data.
story.concept_3_d: Putting the right stock in the right place, at the right time.
story.concept_5_d: Spotting behaviour impossible to guess with the naked eye.
story.close: The value of data isn't only in dashboards: it's in uncovering opportunities invisible to the human eye. That's exactly what I'm trying to spark here, with the data of the Pacific.

act12.thesis: Since 1977, hundreds of tropical systems have crossed the region. Layered season after season, their tracks reveal a pattern — and it is not their number that is most worrying, but their strength.
act12.no_data: Cyclone data not found. Drop the file into public/data/cyclones/ and try again.

viz.map_title: Half a century of cyclones over the Pacific
viz.map_find: Scrub through the seasons — or search a cyclone by name to isolate its track. Past seasons stay as a watermark; the active season draws itself.
viz.bystage_find: How many tracks reached each stage, from weak depression to very intense cyclone.
viz.season_find: The count swings widely from year to year. Science is cautious about frequency: what rises with warming is mainly the share of intense cyclones and the intensity of their rainfall.
viz.exposure_find: Number of cyclones whose track passed within 300 km of the territory, split by peak stage.
viz.cross_find: Each dot is a territory: horizontally, the number of cyclones that passed within 300 km; vertically (log scale), the total people affected by disasters (all causes, SDG source). A cross between our cyclone dataset and the Act 4 “disasters” data.
viz.intensify_find: Share of cyclones reaching tropical-cyclone stage or above, per season (thin line) and as a 5-season rolling average (thick line): from about 38% in the 1970s to over 50% recently, while the number of systems per season stays flat. A caveat: the earliest seasons, before the modern satellite era, likely underestimate intensities.
viz.month_find: Cyclone genesis by month (austral season, July → June, rows) and by decade (columns). The redder a cell, the more cyclones formed that month; green marks the lull. The season peaks from December to April.
viz.wp_find: Each dot is a cyclone: its minimum pressure (horizontal, hPa) against its maximum wind (vertical, knots), coloured by peak stage. The lower the pressure, the higher the wind — the physical relationship that defines a cyclone's intensity.

outro.title: Strength, more than numbers
outro.text: Cyclones are a reminder that the climate does not warm in silence — it strikes. The question now is what the region can still manage to grow, season after season.
